question: A previously healthy 23-year-old man developed progressive headaches, new left-sided weakness, and facial numbness over several months; MRI is shown. Considering his clinical course and the imaging, which near-term complication is he most likely to develop?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the sagittal brain MRI for lesion location and mass effect. I see a large, expansile intraparenchymal-appearing lesion in the posterior fossa centered at the brainstem/pons region that produces notable mass effect on the adjacent CSF spaces: the prepontine cistern and the region of the fourth ventricle appear effaced/compromised by the lesion and the posterior fossa structures are displaced.

Next, I relate these imaging findings to the clinical stem, which describes progressive headaches and new focal brainstem-related deficits (left-sided weakness, facial numbness) over months. Progressive headaches suggest raised intracranial pressure that could result from impaired CSF flow, and the lesion’s location directly overlies the normal CSF outflow pathways at the level of the fourth ventricle/aqueduct.

Then I consider each option against the image and clinical course. Obstructive hydrocephalus is a direct mechanical consequence when a posterior fossa/brainstem mass compresses the fourth ventricle or aqueduct — this matches both the imaging showing effacement of posterior fossa CSF spaces and the progressive headaches. Basilar artery infarction would typically present acutely and is not the most immediate complication suggested by progressive mass effect on CSF flow. Generalized seizures are more likely with cortical lesions; this lesion is centered in the brainstem/posterior fossa, making seizures less likely as the near-term complication. Leptomeningeal dissemination would require imaging evidence of diffuse leptomeningeal/CSF-space tumor spread, which is not seen here; this appears as a single large focal mass producing mass effect.

Therefore, given the lesion’s location and clear mass effect on posterior fossa CSF pathways together with the patient’s progressive headaches, the most likely near-term complication is obstructive hydrocephalus.
caption: MRI of multifocal lesions on presentation. A Axial T2-FLAIR demonstrating a hyperintense round lesion, measuring 2.7 × 2.2 × 1.5 cm (CC × AP × transverse), in the precentral gyrus. Initial measurements prior to presentation at our institution: 2.2 × 1.7 × 1.5 cm. B Left greater than right hyperintensity in the cerebral peduncles on axial T2-FLAIR. C Axial T2-FLAIR demonstrates a heterogenous, hyperintense lesion in the pons, measuring 3.9 × 3.1 × 4.4 cm, with extension into bilateral cerebellopontine cisterns and middle cerebellar peduncles. Encasement of the basilar artery is also present with no obstruction of flow. Initial measurements prior to presentation at our institution: 3.5 × 3.1 × 4.3 cm. D Axial T1 post-contrast image of the pontine lesion demonstrating subtle patchy contrast enhancement. E Sagittal T2 image of the precentral lesion with compression of the central sulcus. F Large heterogenous, T1 hypointense pontine lesion extending from the floor the fourth ventricle to the prepontine cistern ventrally. G Pontine lesion as described with heterogenous hyperintensity on this sagittal T2 slice. H Subtle patchy enhancement is again appreciated within the pontine lesion on this T1 post-contrast sagittal slice
sub_caption: F Large heterogenous, T1 hypointense pontine lesion extending from the floor the fourth ventricle to the prepontine cistern ventrally.
image_context: The patient, a previously healthy 23-year-old man, first presented to an outside hospital with complaints of intermittent diplopia, tinnitus and occipital pain. Due to persistent headache, he underwent an MRI scan which revealed synchronous, non-enhancing, T2/FLAIR hyperintense lesions in the right precentral gyrus and pons as well as increased signal along the right 8th cranial nerve. Initial serial scans were stable and the patient remained in stable condition during that time but eventually presented to our institution 8 months later for a second opinion. By this time, he developed headaches, mild photophobia, left-sided weakness, and left facial numbness. Initial in-house imaging at the time demonstrated a 3.9 × 3.1 × 4.4 cm heterogenous, but primarily T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, infiltrating, and mildly expansile pontine lesion with minimal patchy contrast enhancement and diffusion restriction that was larger in size compared to imaging obtained from the outside hospital. Extension into the floor of the fourth ventricle, compression of the prepontine cistern, and obstructive hydrocephalus was noted. Extension into the middle cerebellar peduncles and encasement of the basilar artery was also noted. MR spectroscopy revealed elevated choline peak and reversal of the choline/NAA ratio, and was overall consistent with a glial tumor. A second infiltrating and expansile, non-enhancing lesion, measuring 2.7 × 2.2 × 1.5 cm, in the right precentral gyrus with similar imaging characteristics to the pontine lesion was also seen and noted to have increased in size compared to imaging studies obtained from the outside hospital (Fig. <xref rid="40478_2021_1265_Fig1_HTML" ref-type="fig">1</xref>). MR perfusion demonstrated low relative cerebral blood volume and flow values, suggestive of a low-grade glioma.). MR perfusion demonstrated low relative cerebral blood volume and flow values, suggestive of a low-grade glioma.Fig. 1MRI of multifocal lesions on presentation. Initial measurements prior to presentation at our institution: 2.2 × 1.7 × 1.5 cm. B Left greater than right hyperintensity in the cerebral peduncles on axial T2-FLAIR. C Axial T2-FLAIR demonstrates a heterogenous, hyperintense lesion in the pons, measuring 3.9 × 3.1 × 4.4 cm, with extension into bilateral cerebellopontine cisterns and middle cerebellar peduncles. Encasement of the basilar artery is also present with no obstruction of flow. Initial measurements prior to presentation at our institution: 3.5 × 3.1 × 4.3 cm. D Axial T1 post-contrast image of the pontine lesion demonstrating subtle patchy contrast enhancement. E Sagittal T2 image of the precentral lesion with compression of the central sulcus. F Large heterogenous, T1 hypointense pontine lesion extending from the floor the fourth ventricle to the prepontine cistern ventrally. G Pontine lesion as described with heterogenous hyperintensity on this sagittal T2 slice. H Subtle patchy enhancement is again appreciated within the pontine lesion on this T1 post-contrast sagittal slice
relevant_image_context: The patient, a previously healthy 23-year-old man, first presented to an outside hospital with complaints of intermittent diplopia, tinnitus and occipital pain. Due to persistent headache, he underwent an MRI scan which revealed synchronous, non-enhancing, T2/FLAIR hyperintense lesions in the right precentral gyrus and pons as well as increased signal along the right 8th cranial nerve. Initial serial scans were stable and the patient remained in stable condition during that time but eventually presented to our institution 8 months later for a second opinion. By this time, he developed headaches, mild photophobia, left-sided weakness, and left facial numbness. Initial in-house imaging at the time demonstrated a 3.9 × 3.1 × 4.4 cm heterogenous, but primarily T1 hypointense, T2 hyperintense, infiltrating, and mildly expansile pontine lesion with minimal patchy contrast enhancement and diffusion restriction that was larger in size compared to imaging obtained from the outside hospital. Extension into the floor of the fourth ventricle, compression of the prepontine cistern, and obstructive hydrocephalus was noted. Extension into the middle cerebellar peduncles and encasement of the basilar artery was also noted. MR spectroscopy revealed elevated choline peak and reversal of the choline/NAA ratio, and was overall consistent with a glial tumor. A second infiltrating and expansile, non-enhancing lesion, measuring 2.7 × 2.2 × 1.5 cm, in the right precentral gyrus with similar imaging characteristics to the pontine lesion was also seen and noted to have increased in size compared to imaging studies obtained from the outside hospital (Fig. 1). MR perfusion demonstrated low relative cerebral blood volume and flow values, suggestive of a low-grade glioma. Fig. 1 MRI of multifocal lesions on presentation. A Axial T2-FLAIR demonstrating a hyperintense round lesion, measuring 2.7 × 2.2 × 1.5 cm (CC × AP × trans­verse), in the precentral gyrus. Initial measurements prior to presentation at our institution: 2.2 × 1.7 × 1.5 cm. B Left greater than right hyperintensity in the cerebral peduncles on axial T2-FLAIR. C Axial T2-FLAIR demonstrates a heterogenous, hyperintense lesion in the pons, measuring 3.9 × 3.1 × 4.4 cm, with extension into bilateral cerebellopontine cisterns and middle cerebellar peduncles. Encasement of the basilar artery is also present with no obstruction of flow. Initial measurements prior to presentation at our institution: 3.5 × 3.1 × 4.3 cm. D Axial T1 post-contrast image of the pontine lesion demonstrating subtle patchy enhancement. E Sagittal T2 image of the precentral lesion with compression of the central sulcus. G Pontine lesion as described with heterogenous hyperintensity on this sagittal T2 slice. H Subtle patchy enhancement is again appreciated within the pontine lesion on this T1 post-contrast sagittal slice.
